schools.summary: Gill: Esau was surnamed Edom, from the red pottage he sold his birthright for to his brother Jacob; an account is given of him and his posterity; the witnesses note Moses opening the record of the generations of Esau, who is Edom — the heading of the genealogy of the rejected brother, set down that God's word concerning him might be seen fulfilled (the Scripture turning aside to record the line of Esau: 'Now these are the generations of Esau, who is Edom'; the naming, 'Esau, who is Edom,' the surname 'Edom,' meaning 'red,' fastened on him from the red pottage for which he had despised and sold his birthright, a name that branded his profanity ever after; the purpose of this chapter, that, before the history goes on to follow Jacob and his sons, the chosen line, it pauses to give the generations of Esau, that God's word might be seen fulfilled; the faithfulness of Scripture in this, that it records even the line of the rejected brother, both because God had promised Esau too should become a nation, and that Israel might know their kinsmen the Edomites, so the chapter opens with the generations of Esau, who is Edom).

themes: Now these are the generations of Esau, who is Edom
the surname Edom branding his profanity in the sold birthright
Scripture recording even the rejected brother's line
that God's promise of a nation from Esau be seen fulfilled

schools.summary: Gill: Esau took his wives of the daughters of Canaan, the posterity of cursed Canaan, most of them being of them though not all; Adah the Hittite and Aholibamah the Hivite; the witnesses note Esau marrying Adah the Hittite and Aholibamah the Hivite, daughters of Canaan — the unhallowed marriages of Esau, who took wives from the cursed and idolatrous Canaanites (the record marking the source of Esau's wives: 'Esau took his wives of the daughters of Canaan; Adah the daughter of Elon the Hittite, and Aholibamah the daughter of Anah the daughter of Zibeon the Hivite'; the contrast with the chosen line, that whereas Abraham and Isaac had charged their sons not to take wives of the Canaanites, and Jacob had gone to Padan-aram for a wife of his kindred, Esau 'took his wives of the daughters of Canaan,' the posterity of cursed Canaan, joining himself to the idolatrous peoples of the land; the grief this had been to Isaac and Rebekah, a mark of Esau's profane and worldly spirit; the difficulty the commentators weigh, that the names of Esau's wives here differ from those given earlier, variously explained as different names for the same women or as later marriages; that Esau's Canaanite marriages set his line apart from the covenant family).

debates.question: Why do Esau's wives' names here differ from those in Genesis 26:34 and 28:9?

sides.position: Different names (or surnames) for the same women — a common ancient practice
Later marriages, or the record reflecting different stages of his household

themes: Esau took his wives of the daughters of Canaan
Adah the daughter of Elon the Hittite, and Aholibamah the daughter of Anah
Esau's unhallowed marriages to the cursed Canaanites
a mark of his profane spirit, setting his line apart

schools.summary: Gill: Bashemath was Ishmael's daughter, the sister of Nebajoth, Ishmael's eldest son, called elsewhere (Genesis 28:9) Mahalath; Charles Ellicott notes the versions weigh the names of Esau's wives; the witnesses note Esau's wife Bashemath, daughter of Ishmael and sister of Nebajoth — the marriage to the daughter of Ishmael, joining Esau to the other rejected line (the record naming a third wife: 'Bashemath Ishmael's daughter, sister of Nebajoth'; the significance of this match, that Esau, besides the Canaanite women, also 'took... Ishmael's daughter,' marrying into the line of Ishmael, the son of Abraham who had been cast out and was not the heir of promise; that Esau took this wife to please his parents, who were grieved at his Canaanite marriages, thinking a daughter of Ishmael more acceptable, yet even this was a joining to a rejected branch, not to the chosen line; the difficulty that this wife is called 'Bashemath' here but 'Mahalath' in 28:9, explained as two names for the same woman; that Esau thus allied himself with both the cursed Canaanites and the rejected Ishmaelites, so Esau takes Bashemath, Ishmael's daughter, to wife).

themes: And Bashemath Ishmael's daughter, sister of Nebajoth
Esau's marriage into the rejected line of Ishmael
taken to please his grieved parents, yet a cast-out branch
allied with both the Canaanites and the Ishmaelites

schools.summary: Gill: Eliphaz, this son of Esau, according to Jerome is the same with him mentioned in the book of Job as one of his friends that came to visit him; the witnesses note Adah bearing Eliphaz to Esau, and Bashemath bearing Reuel — the naming of Esau's firstborn sons, the heads of chief Edomite branches (the record beginning to number Esau's sons: 'Adah bare to Esau Eliphaz; and Bashemath bare Reuel'; the two sons here named, 'Eliphaz,' born of Adah the Hittite, the eldest, who would be the head of a chief Edomite branch and father of the Temanites, and 'Reuel,' born of Bashemath, Ishmael's daughter, another head of a branch; the tradition some of the commentators report, that this 'Eliphaz' was thought by Jerome and others to be the same as Eliphaz the Temanite, the friend of Job, for Job's friend was of Teman in Edom, descended from this line, and whether the same man or no, the connection shows the spread of Esau's house into the lands east and south; that from these sons the dukes and tribes of Edom would descend, the nation growing as God had said, so Esau's sons Eliphaz and Reuel are named).

themes: And Adah bare to Esau Eliphaz; and Bashemath bare Reuel
Esau's sons, heads of chief Edomite branches
Eliphaz thought by some the same as Job's friend the Temanite
the nation growing from Esau as God had promised

schools.summary: Gill: in this genealogy mention is made of another Korah among the sons of Eliphaz, which Jarchi thinks is the same with this; the witnesses note Aholibamah bearing Jeush, Jaalam, and Korah, these being Esau's sons born in the land of Canaan — the completing of the list of Esau's five sons, all born in Canaan before his removal (the record closing the count of Esau's sons: 'Aholibamah bare Jeush, and Jaalam, and Korah: these are the sons of Esau, which were born unto him in the land of Canaan'; the three sons of Aholibamah, the Hivite wife, 'Jeush, and Jaalam, and Korah,' who with Eliphaz and Reuel made the five sons of Esau; the summary, 'these are the sons of Esau, which were born unto him in the land of Canaan,' that all five were born in Canaan before Esau removed to Seir, marking the beginning of his house while still in the land; the small difficulty the commentators weigh, that a 'Korah' appears both here among Aholibamah's sons and later among the dukes of Eliphaz's line, variously explained; that these five sons were the fountains of the Edomite tribes, so Esau's five sons, born in Canaan, are numbered).

themes: Aholibamah bare Jeush, and Jaalam, and Korah
these are the sons of Esau, which were born unto him in the land of Canaan
Esau's five sons completed, all born in Canaan
the fountains of the Edomite tribes, his house begun in the land

schools.summary: Adam Clarke and Gill note Esau took his whole house and substance which he had got in the land of Canaan and went into the country of Seir, away from the face of his brother Jacob; the witnesses note Esau gathering his wives, sons, daughters, household, cattle, and all his substance, and going into the country away from Jacob — the voluntary removal of Esau from Canaan, leaving the land of promise to his brother (Esau gathering all and departing: 'Esau took his wives, and his sons, and his daughters, and all the persons of his house, and his cattle... and went into the country from the face of his brother Jacob'; the completeness of the removal, that Esau took 'all the persons of his house,' 'his cattle,' 'all his beasts,' and 'all his substance, which he had got in the land of Canaan,' and 'went into the country,' to Seir, 'from the face of his brother Jacob'; the providence and meaning of it, that Esau, of his own will, withdrew from Canaan and left the land of promise to Jacob, so that the inheritance God had appointed to the chosen line was not contested; the contrast, that Esau, the worldly man, sought a settled possession of his own elsewhere, while Jacob remained a pilgrim in the promised land, so Esau removes with all his house away from Jacob).

themes: Esau took his wives, and his sons, and his daughters, and all the persons of his house
and went into the country from the face of his brother Jacob
Esau's voluntary removal, leaving Canaan to Jacob
the worldly man seeking his own possession, Jacob remaining a pilgrim

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: the land wherein they were strangers could not bear them; Gill: their riches were more than that they might dwell together, so it was proper to part, as Abraham and Lot had done before; the witnesses note that the riches of Esau and Jacob were too great for them to dwell together, the land not able to bear them because of their cattle — the parting for want of room, the same cause that had once separated Abraham and Lot (the record giving the reason for Esau's removal: 'their riches were more than that they might dwell together; and the land wherein they were strangers could not bear them because of their cattle'; the cause, that both brothers, Esau and Jacob, were now grown so 'rich' in flocks and herds that 'the land wherein they were strangers could not bear them,' there not being pasture enough for both their cattle in the same place; the likeness to an earlier parting, that this was the very reason Abraham and Lot had separated, abundance of substance requiring room, and so a peaceful parting; that here the parting was without strife, Esau withdrawing to Seir; God's providence in their very wealth, which served to separate the lines and settle each in its appointed place, so the brothers part for want of room).

themes: their riches were more than that they might dwell together
the land wherein they were strangers could not bear them because of their cattle
the brothers parting for want of room
the same cause that once separated Abraham and Lot, God's providence in their wealth

schools.summary: Gill: Esau, before said to be in the land of Seir, now dwelt in a mount of that name, from which, driving the Horites, he seized upon and dwelt in it; the witnesses note Esau dwelling in Mount Seir, with the note that Esau is Edom — the settling of Esau in his own mountain land, the fixing of Edom in Seir (the record marking where Esau settled: 'Thus dwelt Esau in mount Seir: Esau is Edom'; the place, 'mount Seir,' the hill-country south and east of the Dead Sea, where Esau, driving out the Horites who had dwelt there, seized and possessed the land for himself and his seed; the repeated identification, 'Esau is Edom,' twice in the chapter the Scripture pressing that Esau and Edom are one, that the nation of Edom sprang wholly from Esau; the fulfillment in this, that God's word to Rebekah, 'two nations are in thy womb,' was now coming to pass, Esau become the father of the Edomite nation, settled in Seir, while Jacob's seed should be the people of God, so Esau dwells in Mount Seir, the father of Edom).

debates.question: What does Esau's settling in Seir as Edom fulfill?

sides.position: God's word to Rebekah, 'two nations are in thy womb' (Genesis 25:23) — Esau becoming the father of the Edomite nation, distinct from Jacob's chosen line

themes: Thus dwelt Esau in mount Seir: Esau is Edom
Esau settling in his mountain land, driving out the Horites
the repeated 'Esau is Edom,' Esau the father of the nation
God's word of two nations from Rebekah's womb coming to pass

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: here only the names of Esau's posterity are recorded, that the truth of God's promise to Abraham (that he should be the father of many nations) and to Esau (that he should have a dwelling and live by his sword) might appear; Gill: these are the posterity of Esau, his children and grandchildren, in Seir; the witnesses note Moses opening again the generations of Esau, the father of the Edomites in Mount Seir — the renewed heading, marking Esau now as the father of a nation settled in its own land (the record taking up the line afresh, now in Seir: 'these are the generations of Esau the father of the Edomites in mount Seir'; the difference from the first heading, that there Esau's sons born in Canaan, here 'the generations of Esau the father of the Edomites in mount Seir,' the line now traced as a settled nation in its own mountain land; the title 'the father of the Edomites,' that Esau is owned the founder of a whole people sprung from his loins; why these names are recorded at all, as Matthew Henry notes, that 'the truth of God's promise' might appear, both the promise to Abraham that kings and nations should come of his seed, and the word to Esau himself that he should have a dwelling and live by his sword, so the genealogy of Esau the father of Edom is taken up in Seir).

themes: these are the generations of Esau the father of the Edomites
in mount Seir
the renewed heading, Esau the father of a settled nation
the names recorded that the truth of God's promise might appear

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: these are the names of Esau's sons, Eliphaz son of Adah, and Reuel son of Bashemath; Gill: in this and the following verses an account is given of Esau's sons and his sons' sons; the witnesses note the names of Esau's sons, Eliphaz of Adah and Reuel of Bashemath, repeated as the record turns to the grandsons — the renaming of the two chief sons, the heads from whom the Edomite branches descend (the record naming again the two principal sons before tracing their offspring: 'These are the names of Esau's sons; Eliphaz the son of Adah the wife of Esau, Reuel the son of Bashemath the wife of Esau'; the purpose of the repetition, that, having named Esau's five sons, the record now singles out the two by Adah and Bashemath, 'Eliphaz' and 'Reuel,' as the heads from whom the grandsons and the chief dukes of Edom would descend; the care of the Scripture in tracing the line through the mothers, 'the wife of Esau' twice named, that the descent be clear; that from these two sons especially the tribes of Edom branched out, so Esau's two chief sons, Eliphaz and Reuel, are named as the record turns to their offspring).

themes: These are the names of Esau's sons; Eliphaz the son of Adah
Reuel the son of Bashemath the wife of Esau
the two chief sons renamed as the record turns to the grandsons
the heads from whom the chief Edomite branches descend

schools.summary: Gill: Teman was the firstborn of Eliphaz, and from him the city of Teman in Edom or Idumea had its name, whence Eliphaz is called the Temanite; four more sons are named, Omar, Zepho, Gatam, and Kenaz; the witnesses note the five sons of Eliphaz, Teman, Omar, Zepho, Gatam, and Kenaz — the grandsons of Esau by Eliphaz, heads of Edomite clans, Teman among them (the record numbering the offspring of Eliphaz: 'the sons of Eliphaz were Teman, Omar, Zepho, and Gatam, and Kenaz'; the chief among them, 'Teman' the firstborn, from whom 'the city of Teman in Edom' took its name, a place noted in Scripture for its wise men, and whence 'Eliphaz the Temanite,' Job's friend, was called; the rest, 'Omar, Zepho, Gatam, and Kenaz,' each the head of an Edomite clan; the steady growth of Esau's house, the grandsons now multiplying into the families that would form the dukedoms of Edom; that the Scripture's care to name even these shows the truth of God's word that Esau should become a people, so the five sons of Eliphaz, heads of clans, are named).

themes: the sons of Eliphaz were Teman, Omar, Zepho
and Gatam, and Kenaz
the five sons of Eliphaz, heads of Edomite clans
Teman the firstborn, whence the city and Job's friend the Temanite

schools.summary: Gill: Timna was the sister of Lotan, the eldest son of Seir the Horite; she bare Amalek to Eliphaz, from whom came the Amalekites, the great enemies of Israel; the witnesses note Timna, concubine to Eliphaz, bearing Amalek, the sons being of Adah Esau's wife — the birth of Amalek, the father of Israel's bitter foe, sprung from Esau's line (the record noting a son born of a concubine: 'Timna was concubine to Eliphaz Esau's son; and she bare to Eliphaz Amalek: these were the sons of Adah Esau's wife'; who this son was, 'Amalek,' born to Eliphaz by his concubine Timna, the father of the Amalekites, that fierce and cruel nation which would become the first and most bitter enemy of Israel, against whom God swore perpetual war; the solemn note in this, that the foe who would fall upon Israel in the wilderness sprang from Esau's house, the kindred turned to enmity; the gathering of all these, sons, grandson, and concubine's son, under 'the sons of Adah Esau's wife,' tracing the whole branch to its mother, so Amalek, the father of Israel's enemy, is born in Esau's line).

debates.question: What is the solemn significance of Amalek's place in Esau's line?

sides.position: Amalek, father of the Amalekites, became Israel's first and most bitter foe (Exodus 17:8-16; Deuteronomy 25:17-19) — the kindred of Esau turned to perpetual enmity against the chosen line

themes: Timna was concubine to Eliphaz Esau's son; and she bare to Eliphaz Amalek
these were the sons of Adah Esau's wife
Amalek, father of Israel's bitter foe, sprung from Esau's house
the kindred turned to perpetual enmity against the chosen line

schools.summary: Jamieson, Fausset and Brown note that, notwithstanding the ill usage Jacob had received from Esau, the Edomite line is faithfully recorded; Gill: Reuel, another son of Esau, had four sons, Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah; the witnesses note Reuel's four sons, the sons of Bashemath Esau's wife — the grandsons of Esau by Reuel, the second branch of the Edomite house (the record numbering the offspring of Reuel: 'these are the sons of Reuel; Nahath, and Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah: these were the sons of Bashemath Esau's wife'; the four grandsons born of Reuel, the son of Bashemath, Ishmael's daughter, heads of clans in the second branch of Esau's house; the tracing again to the mother, 'the sons of Bashemath Esau's wife,' that the descent be ordered and clear; the faithfulness of the Scripture, as JFB observe, in recording even this line of the brother who had wronged Jacob, neither hiding nor slighting the kindred of Israel; the steady multiplying of Esau's house, branch after branch, so the four sons of Reuel, heads of clans, are named).

themes: these are the sons of Reuel; Nahath, and Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah
these were the sons of Bashemath Esau's wife
Reuel's four sons, the second branch of Esau's house
Scripture faithfully recording even the line of the brother who wronged Jacob

schools.summary: Gill: Aholibamah was the daughter of Anah, the daughter of Zibeon, Esau's wife, who bare Jeush, Jaalam, and Korah; the witnesses note Aholibamah, daughter of Anah, bearing to Esau Jeush, Jaalam, and Korah — the third branch of Esau's house, the sons of the Hivite wife (the record completing the three branches with Aholibamah's sons: 'these were the sons of Aholibamah, the daughter of Anah the daughter of Zibeon, Esau's wife: and she bare to Esau Jeush, and Jaalam, and Korah'; that, where Adah and Bashemath's lines were traced through grandsons, Aholibamah's three sons, 'Jeush, and Jaalam, and Korah,' are named directly as her sons, being themselves the heads of their branches; the careful tracing of Aholibamah's descent, 'the daughter of Anah the daughter of Zibeon,' the Horite line, linking Esau's house by marriage to the earlier inhabitants of Seir; that these three, with the lines of Eliphaz and Reuel, made up the heads of the house of Esau, so Aholibamah's three sons complete the branches of Esau's house).

themes: these were the sons of Aholibamah, the daughter of Anah the daughter of Zibeon, Esau's wife
and she bare to Esau Jeush, and Jaalam, and Korah
the third branch of Esau's house, the Hivite wife's sons
her descent linking Esau's house to the Horites of Seir

schools.summary: Gill: Ben Melech says the difference between a duke and a king was that a king is crowned and a duke is not; Jarchi interprets the word of heads of families or tribes; the witnesses note the dukes of the sons of Esau, the sons of Eliphaz, duke Teman, duke Omar, duke Zepho, duke Kenaz — the rise of the Edomite dukes, the heads of clans now grown to chieftains and rulers (the record advancing from sons to dukes: 'These were dukes of the sons of Esau: the sons of Eliphaz the firstborn son of Esau; duke Teman, duke Omar, duke Zepho, duke Kenaz'; the meaning of 'duke,' not a crowned king but, as the commentators explain, a head of a family or tribe, a chieftain ruling over a clan, the form of government in Edom before the kings arose; the dignity of the line, that Esau's grandsons by Eliphaz had now grown into 'dukes,' each ruling his clan, so that the house of Esau was become a settled and ordered people with princes of its own; the fulfillment again of God's word, that Esau should be a nation, here seen in the rise of these chieftains, so the dukes of the sons of Eliphaz are named).

themes: These were dukes of the sons of Esau: the sons of Eliphaz the firstborn
duke Teman, duke Omar, duke Zepho, duke Kenaz
the rise of the Edomite dukes, chieftains ruling clans
the house of Esau an ordered people, God's word fulfilled

schools.summary: Gill: among the sons of Eliphaz is reckoned Duke Korah, not before mentioned among his sons in v.11 (he was a son of Aholibamah, v.5), and left out in the Samaritan version, variously explained as a grandson or later name; the witnesses note the dukes Korah, Gatam, and Amalek, the dukes of Eliphaz in Edom, the sons of Adah — the completing of the dukes of the first branch, Amalek among them (the record finishing the dukes of Eliphaz: 'Duke Korah, duke Gatam, and duke Amalek: these are the dukes that came of Eliphaz in the land of Edom; these were the sons of Adah'; the difficulty the commentators weigh, that 'duke Korah' is here reckoned among the dukes of Eliphaz though not named among Eliphaz's sons, and is left out in the Samaritan; that 'duke Amalek' appears here, the head of the Amalekites, a duke in Edom, the same whose people would war on Israel; the summary, 'the dukes that came of Eliphaz... the sons of Adah,' gathering all the first branch under their mother; the full count of the dukes of Adah's line, so the dukes of Eliphaz, Amalek among them, are completed).

themes: Duke Korah, duke Gatam, and duke Amalek
these are the dukes that came of Eliphaz... these were the sons of Adah
the dukes of Eliphaz's line completed, Amalek among them
gathered under Adah their mother, the Korah difficulty weighed

schools.summary: Gill: these are the sons of Reuel, already mentioned, now reckoned as dukes; the witnesses note the dukes Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah, the dukes of Reuel in Edom, the sons of Bashemath — the dukes of the second branch, the chieftains of Reuel's line (the record naming the dukes of the second branch: 'these are the sons of Reuel Esau's son; duke Nahath, duke Zerah, duke Shammah, duke Mizzah: these are the dukes that came of Reuel in the land of Edom; these are the sons of Bashemath Esau's wife'; that Reuel's four sons are now reckoned as 'dukes,' each grown into a chieftain ruling his clan 'in the land of Edom'; the order and the parallel, that, as with Eliphaz's line, so with Reuel's, the sons are made dukes and all gathered under their mother 'Bashemath Esau's wife'; the steady, ordered growth of Esau's house into a people governed by its dukes, each branch yielding its chieftains; the faithfulness of the record in numbering all alike, so the dukes of Reuel, the second branch, are named).

themes: these are the sons of Reuel Esau's son; duke Nahath, duke Zerah, duke Shammah, duke Mizzah
these are the sons of Bashemath Esau's wife
the dukes of Reuel's line, the second branch
the ordered growth of Esau's house into a people of dukes

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: Aholibamah's three sons are themselves dukes, while of the other wives only the grandsons rose to that rank; Gill: these are the sons of Aholibamah, Esau's wife, now reckoned as dukes; the witnesses note the dukes Jeush, Jaalam, and Korah, the dukes of Aholibamah, the daughter of Anah — the third branch's dukes, the sons themselves chieftains (the record naming the dukes of the third branch: 'these are the sons of Aholibamah Esau's wife; duke Jeush, duke Jaalam, duke Korah: these were the dukes that came of Aholibamah the daughter of Anah, Esau's wife'; the difference Ellicott marks, that in Aholibamah's line the three 'sons' themselves are reckoned dukes, whereas in the lines of Adah and Bashemath it was the grandsons who rose to that rank; that this completes the dukes of the house of Esau, the three branches of his three wives all yielding their chieftains; that the whole genealogy thus shows Esau's house grown into an ordered nation of dukes, each clan with its head; the fulfillment of God's word that Esau should be a great people, so the dukes of Aholibamah complete the dukes of Esau).

themes: these are the sons of Aholibamah Esau's wife; duke Jeush, duke Jaalam, duke Korah
these were the dukes that came of Aholibamah the daughter of Anah
Aholibamah's three sons themselves reckoned dukes
completing the dukes of Esau's house, the three branches all yielding chieftains

schools.summary: Gill: this is the conclusion of the account of Esau's posterity and their dukes, repeating that Esau is Edom; the witnesses note the summary that these are the sons of Esau, who is Edom, and these their dukes — the sealing of the record of Esau's house, the line and its chieftains gathered under the name Edom (the record closing the account of Esau's own descendants: 'These are the sons of Esau, who is Edom, and these are their dukes'; the summing word, that all the foregoing, 'the sons of Esau' and 'their dukes,' are gathered into one, the whole house of Esau, 'who is Edom,' numbered and sealed; the third repetition of 'who is Edom' in the chapter, pressing again that Esau and the nation of Edom are one; that here the line of Esau himself is complete, before the record turns aside to the older inhabitants of Seir, the Horites; that the whole stands as a witness to God's faithfulness, that Esau, the rejected brother, was yet made a nation with princes of his own, as God had said, so the record of Esau's sons and dukes is sealed under the name Edom).

themes: These are the sons of Esau, who is Edom, and these are their dukes
the sealing summary of Esau's house under the name Edom
the third repetition of 'who is Edom,' Esau and the nation one
a witness that the rejected brother was made a nation with princes

schools.summary: Gill: these are the sons of Seir the Horite, who inhabited the land before it was inhabited by Esau and his posterity, as the Targum of Jonathan adds; the witnesses note the sons of Seir the Horite who inhabited the land, Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, and Anah — the record of the older inhabitants of Seir, the Horites whom Esau dispossessed (the Scripture turning aside to the people who held the land before Esau: 'these are the sons of Seir the Horite, who inhabited the land; Lotan, and Shobal, and Zibeon, and Anah'; who these were, 'Seir the Horite' and his sons, the 'Horites,' cave-dwellers, who 'inhabited the land' of Seir before Esau came, the original possessors of the mountain country; why their genealogy is given, both because Esau's house had intermarried with them, Aholibamah being of this line, so that the two peoples were joined, and because Esau's seed dispossessed them and took their land, so that the record of the Horites belongs to the history of how Edom came to be; that the Horite chiefs are numbered as the Edomite were, that the land's history be complete, so the sons of Seir the Horite are named).

themes: these are the sons of Seir the Horite, who inhabited the land
Lotan, and Shobal, and Zibeon, and Anah
the older inhabitants of Seir before Esau
their line given, for Esau's house joined and then dispossessed them

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan, with the four before, made the seven sons of Seir, the dukes of the Horites; Gill: these three others, with the four before mentioned, made seven; the witnesses note Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan, the dukes of the Horites, the children of Seir in Edom — the completing of Seir's seven sons, the chieftains of the Horites in the land (the record finishing the sons of Seir: 'Dishon, and Ezer, and Dishan: these are the dukes of the Horites, the children of Seir in the land of Edom'; the count, that these three, 'Dishon, and Ezer, and Dishan,' with the four before, made up 'the seven sons of Seir,' the heads and 'dukes of the Horites'; that the Horites, like the Edomites, were ruled by dukes, chieftains of their clans, 'in the land of Edom,' so the land is here called by the name it bore in Moses' day though it was the Horites' before Esau; the parallel between the two peoples, both ordered under dukes, the older now to be supplanted by the younger; that the record numbers the Horite chiefs faithfully, that the history of the land be full, so the seven sons of Seir, the dukes of the Horites, are completed).

themes: And Dishon, and Ezer, and Dishan
these are the dukes of the Horites, the children of Seir in the land of Edom
Seir's seven sons completed, the Horite dukes
the older people, ordered under dukes, soon supplanted by Esau

schools.summary: Gill: the first of Lotan's children, Hori, seems to have his name from the general name of the tribe or nation, and the other, Hemam, is called Homam elsewhere; Lotan's sister was Timna; the witnesses note the children of Lotan, Hori and Hemam, and Lotan's sister Timna — the offspring of Seir's eldest son, and the naming of Timna, who linked the Horite line to Esau's house (the record tracing the line of Lotan: 'the children of Lotan were Hori and Hemam; and Lotan's sister was Timna'; the sons, 'Hori and Hemam,' Homam elsewhere, the offspring of Lotan, Seir's firstborn; the special mention of 'Lotan's sister... Timna,' the same Timna who became concubine to Eliphaz, Esau's son, and bare Amalek; the significance the commentators draw, that here the Horite and Edomite lines are seen joined, Timna of the Horites passing into Esau's house, so that the two peoples were knit together by marriage even as they were rivals for the land; the care of the Scripture in tracing these connections, that the history of Edom and its earlier inhabitants be one, so Lotan's children and his sister Timna are named).

themes: the children of Lotan were Hori and Hemam
and Lotan's sister was Timna
Lotan's line, and Timna who bare Amalek to Eliphaz
the Horite and Edomite lines joined by marriage

schools.summary: Gill: these were the sons of Shobal, the second son of Seir, who had the five following, Alvan, Manahath, Ebal, Shepho, and Onam; the witnesses note the five children of Shobal, heads of Horite clans — the offspring of Seir's second son (the record numbering the line of Shobal: 'the children of Shobal were these; Alvan, and Manahath, and Ebal, Shepho, and Onam'; the five sons of 'Shobal,' the second son of Seir the Horite, each the head of a Horite clan; the steady tracing of the Horite branches, parallel to the tracing of the Edomite, that the older people too had its families and chiefs, ordered as the younger; that the Scripture numbers these earlier inhabitants with the same care it gives the line of Esau, that the full history of Seir be told; that these clans would in time be absorbed or supplanted as Esau's house grew, so the five children of Shobal are named).

themes: the children of Shobal were these; Alvan, and Manahath, and Ebal
Shepho, and Onam
Shobal's five children, heads of Horite clans
the older people's branches traced with the same care as Esau's

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: this was that Anah who found the 'yemim' in the wilderness, a word variously rendered 'mules' or 'hot springs,' much disputed; Gill: Zibeon was the third son of Seir, who had two sons, Ajah and Anah; the witnesses note the children of Zibeon, Ajah and Anah, and that this Anah found the mules (or hot springs) in the wilderness while feeding the asses — the line of Zibeon and the much-disputed note of Anah's discovery (the record adding a rare detail to a name: 'the children of Zibeon; both Ajah, and Anah: this was that Anah that found the mules in the wilderness, as he fed the asses of Zibeon his father'; the two sons of Zibeon, 'Ajah, and Anah,' and the singling out of 'that Anah' by a memorable deed; the great debate over the word rendered 'mules,' Hebrew yemim, that some with the older versions take it as 'mules,' Anah being the first to breed mules by crossing horse and ass, while many with Clarke render it 'hot springs' or 'warm waters,' and yet others 'giants' or other things; that, whatever the exact sense, the note marks Anah as a man remembered for a discovery made while humbly tending his father's asses, so the line of Zibeon and the noted Anah are named).

debates.question: What did Anah find in the wilderness (Hebrew 'yemim')?

sides.position: 'Mules' — Anah was the first to breed mules by crossing the horse and ass (so the older versions and Jewish tradition)
'Hot springs' or warm waters — Anah discovered the hot springs of the region (so Clarke and many moderns)

themes: the children of Zibeon; both Ajah, and Anah
this was that Anah that found the mules in the wilderness
the line of Zibeon and the disputed note of Anah's discovery
'yemim' rendered mules or hot springs, much debated

schools.summary: Gill: the children of Anah were Dishon and Aholibamah the daughter of Anah; the witnesses note the children of Anah, Dishon his son and Aholibamah his daughter — the line of Anah, the father of Esau's wife Aholibamah (the record naming Anah's children: 'the children of Anah were these; Dishon, and Aholibamah the daughter of Anah'; the two children, a son, 'Dishon,' who would head a clan, and a daughter, 'Aholibamah the daughter of Anah'; the significance of this daughter, that she is the same 'Aholibamah' whom Esau took to wife, so that here the record shows the very point where the Horite and Edomite lines met, Esau marrying into the house of Seir the Horite; that thus the older inhabitants and the new were knit by this marriage, the daughter of a Horite chief becoming the mother of three of Esau's dukes; the care of the Scripture in marking the connection, the genealogy of the Horites here touching the genealogy of Esau, so Anah's children, Dishon and Aholibamah, are named, the link between the two houses).

themes: the children of Anah were these; Dishon
and Aholibamah the daughter of Anah
Anah's daughter Aholibamah, the same Esau took to wife
the point where the Horite and Edomite lines met

schools.summary: Gill: these are the sons not of Dishon the son of Anah, but of Dishon the son of Seir; they are the four following, Hemdan, Eshban, Ithran, and Cheran; the witnesses note the four children of Dishon, the son of Seir, heads of Horite clans (the record numbering Dishon's children: 'these are the children of Dishon; Hemdan, and Eshban, and Ithran, and Cheran'; the care the commentators take to distinguish, that this 'Dishon' is the son of Seir, one of the seven, not the Dishon who was the son of Anah, the two being different men of the same name; the four sons, 'Hemdan, and Eshban, and Ithran, and Cheran,' heads of Horite clans in the line of Seir; the steady completing of the Horite genealogy, each of Seir's sons yielding his offspring, the older people fully numbered; that the Scripture's exactness, even in distinguishing two men named Dishon, shows the faithfulness of the record, so the four children of Dishon, son of Seir, are named).

themes: these are the children of Dishon; Hemdan, and Eshban
and Ithran, and Cheran
the four children of Dishon, son of Seir
carefully distinguished from the other Dishon, son of Anah

schools.summary: Gill: Ezer was another son of Seir, who had the following sons, Bilhan, Zaavan, and Akan; the witnesses note the three children of Ezer, heads of Horite clans (the record numbering Ezer's children: 'The children of Ezer are these; Bilhan, and Zaavan, and Akan'; the three sons of 'Ezer,' the sixth of Seir's seven sons, 'Bilhan, and Zaavan, and Akan,' each the head of a Horite clan; the steady, unbroken numbering of the Horite line, son after son and clan after clan, that the genealogy of the earlier inhabitants of Seir be complete; that the Scripture gives the same orderly account of this rejected people as of the chosen, neither slighting nor passing over them; that these clans, though now flourishing, would in time give way to Esau's growing house, so the three children of Ezer are named).

themes: The children of Ezer are these; Bilhan
and Zaavan, and Akan
the three children of Ezer, the sixth son of Seir
the unbroken numbering of the older Horite line

schools.summary: Gill: Dishan was the last of the seven sons of Seir, and had two sons, Uz and Aran; the witnesses note the two children of Dishan, Uz and Aran, closing the Horite genealogy (the record numbering the last of Seir's sons: 'The children of Dishan are these; Uz, and Aran'; the two sons of 'Dishan,' the last of Seir's seven sons, 'Uz, and Aran,' heads of Horite clans; the interest some find in the name 'Uz,' that it was also the name of the land where Job dwelt, and some have connected the two, though the land of Uz is variously placed; that with Dishan's children the genealogy of the Horites is complete, all seven sons of Seir and their offspring numbered; the fullness of the record, that the history of the earlier people of Seir be told before the account of the kings of Edom, so the two children of Dishan close the line of the Horites).

themes: The children of Dishan are these; Uz
and Aran
the two children of Dishan, the seventh son of Seir
closing the Horite genealogy, the name Uz noted as Job's land

schools.summary: Gill: these are the dukes that came of the Horites, not that succeeded one another as the kings next mentioned did, but were together at the same time, heads of their respective tribes; the witnesses note the dukes of the Horites, duke Lotan, duke Shobal, duke Zibeon, duke Anah — the summing of the Horite chieftains, the heads of the older people ruling together (the record gathering the Horite dukes: 'These are the dukes that came of the Horites; duke Lotan, duke Shobal, duke Zibeon, duke Anah'; the manner of their rule, as the commentators explain, that these dukes did not 'succeed one another,' reigning in turn as the kings of Edom would, but ruled 'together at the same time,' each the head of his own tribe or clan, a government of contemporary chieftains; that the Horites, like the Edomites, were ordered under these dukes, the seven sons of Seir each a chief; that the record names them with the same dignity given the dukes of Esau, the older people's chieftains numbered before the land passed to the younger, so the dukes of the Horites, ruling together, are named).

themes: These are the dukes that came of the Horites; duke Lotan, duke Shobal
duke Zibeon, duke Anah
the dukes of the Horites ruling together as contemporary chieftains
not succeeding one another like the kings, the older people ordered

schools.summary: Gill: these are the dukes that came of Hori, the ancestor of Seir, whence he is called the Horite; the witnesses note the dukes Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan, the dukes that came of Hori in the land of Seir — the completing of the seven Horite dukes, the chieftains of the older people in their land (the record finishing the Horite dukes: 'Duke Dishon, duke Ezer, duke Dishan: these are the dukes that came of Hori, among their dukes in the land of Seir'; the last three dukes, 'Dishon, Ezer, Dishan,' completing the seven heads of the Horites 'in the land of Seir'; the name 'Hori,' the ancestor or the general name of the tribe whence the people were called 'Horites'; that this closes the whole genealogy of the older inhabitants of Seir, the seven sons of Seir, their offspring, and their dukes all numbered, before the record turns to the kings who reigned in Edom; the completeness of the account, that the history of the land and both its peoples, the Horites who held it and the Edomites who took it, be fully told, so the seven dukes of the Horites are completed).

themes: Duke Dishon, duke Ezer, duke Dishan
these are the dukes that came of Hori... in the land of Seir
the seven Horite dukes completed, the older people's rule
closing the land's earlier history before the kings of Edom

schools.summary: Gill: these reigned in the land afterwards called Edom, for it was not so called when these kings began; the note 'before there reigned any king over the children of Israel' is much discussed; the witnesses note Moses recording the kings that reigned in Edom before any king reigned over the children of Israel — the rise of kings in Edom, and the famous note that they reigned before Israel had kings (the record advancing from dukes to kings: 'these are the kings that reigned in the land of Edom, before there reigned any king over the children of Israel'; the order of Edom's rise, that Edom, the worldly nation, came early to the dignity of kings, having a line of monarchs while Israel, the chosen people, was yet without a king, ruled by God himself and later by judges; the much-debated phrase, 'before there reigned any king over the children of Israel,' some taking it that Moses, writing prophetically, foresaw that Israel would have kings, as God had promised Jacob, 'kings shall come out of thy loins,' and notes that Edom got there first, others thinking the words point to a later hand written after Israel had kings; that the simplest reading is Moses' own, looking forward by the spirit of prophecy to the kings God had promised, so Edom's kings reigned before Israel's, the worldly nation rising first).

debates.question: How is 'before there reigned any king over the children of Israel' to be understood?

sides.position: Moses' own prophetic note — foreseeing the kings God had promised Jacob (Genesis 35:11; 17:6), he observes that Edom came to monarchy first, while Israel waited
Some critics take the words as a later editorial addition, written after Israel had kings

themes: these are the kings that reigned in the land of Edom
before there reigned any king over the children of Israel
Edom's kings reigning before Israel had any
the worldly nation rising early to monarchy while the chosen waited

schools.summary: Gill: his name was not Balac, as the Septuagint version has it (which might lead one to think of Balak king of Moab), nor is this the same with Balaam the son of Beor the prophet; the witnesses note Bela the son of Beor reigning in Edom, his city being Dinhabah — the first king of Edom named, the beginning of the Edomite monarchy (the record naming the first king: 'Bela the son of Beor reigned in Edom: and the name of his city was Dinhabah'; the first of the line, 'Bela the son of Beor,' reigning in Edom, with 'his city... Dinhabah,' his royal seat; the care the commentators take to distinguish, that this 'Bela the son of Beor' is not to be confused with 'Balak king of Moab,' nor with 'Balaam the son of Beor,' the prophet, though the names are like; the manner of the Edomite kingship, that the crown did not pass from father to son but from one to another, each king of a different city, an elective or unsettled monarchy rather than a fixed dynasty; the beginning here of the line of eight kings who reigned in Edom, so Bela, the first king of Edom, reigns in Dinhabah).

themes: And Bela the son of Beor reigned in Edom
and the name of his city was Dinhabah
Bela the son of Beor, the first king of Edom
the monarchy begun, the crown passing not by dynasty

schools.summary: Gill: how long Bela reigned is not known, nor whether he left sons; if he did, they did not succeed him in the throne, for Jobab of another city reigned in his stead; Adam Clarke notes some have identified Jobab with Job, though this is uncertain; the witnesses note Bela dying and Jobab the son of Zerah of Bozrah reigning in his stead — the second king of Edom, the crown passing to another house and city (the record continuing the line: 'Bela died, and Jobab the son of Zerah of Bozrah reigned in his stead'; the manner of the succession, that Bela 'died' and 'Jobab' reigned 'in his stead,' but Jobab was 'the son of Zerah of Bozrah,' of another family and another city, not Bela's son; that this confirms the nature of the Edomite kingship already seen, an unsettled or elective monarchy where the crown passed from house to house, not by inheritance; the tradition some report, that this 'Jobab' was thought by certain ancients to be the same as Job, the man of Uz, though this is uncertain and much doubted; the steady recording of king after king, the worldly greatness of Edom rising while Israel was yet a household, so Jobab of Bozrah reigns in Bela's stead).

themes: And Bela died, and Jobab the son of Zerah of Bozrah reigned in his stead
Jobab of Bozrah, the second king, of another house and city
confirming the elective Edomite monarchy
some ancients identifying this Jobab with Job, though uncertain

schools.summary: Gill: Jobab died, and Husham of the land of the Temanites reigned in his stead; the witnesses note Jobab dying and Husham of the land of the Temanites reigning in his stead — the third king of Edom, again of another region (the line continuing: 'Jobab died, and Husham of the land of Temani reigned in his stead'; the third king, 'Husham of the land of Temani,' the land of the Temanites, the south of Edom named from Teman, Eliphaz's firstborn, who reigned after Jobab; again the mark of the unsettled monarchy, that Husham was 'of the land of Temani,' of yet another part of Edom, not of Jobab's house or city, the crown passing once more to a different region and family; the steady succession of these kings, each from a different place, the dignity of the kingship moving among the clans of Edom; the commentators' reckoning of the dates, placing these reigns in the centuries after Abraham, the worldly kingdom of Edom flourishing while the chosen line was still few, so Husham of Teman reigns in Jobab's stead).

themes: And Jobab died, and Husham of the land of Temani reigned in his stead
Husham of Teman, the third king, of another region
the crown moving among the clans of Edom
Edom flourishing while the chosen line was still few

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: Hadad smote Midian in the field of Moab, a warlike king whose deed is recorded; Gill: Husham died, and Hadad the son of Bedad reigned, his city being Avith; the witnesses note Husham dying and Hadad the son of Bedad, who smote Midian in the field of Moab, reigning in his stead, his city being Avith — the fourth king of Edom, a warrior who defeated Midian (the line continuing with a king of war: 'Hadad the son of Bedad, who smote Midian in the field of Moab, reigned in his stead: and the name of his city was Avith'; the distinguishing deed of this fourth king, 'Hadad the son of Bedad,' that he 'smote Midian in the field of Moab,' a victory in battle over the Midianites in the territory of Moab, the one warlike exploit the record pauses to name among these kings; his city, 'Avith,' his royal seat, again a different place; that this glimpse of war shows the kingdom of Edom active and strong among its neighbours, contending and conquering, a worldly power in its prime; that the crown again passed to a king of another house and city, the elective pattern holding, so Hadad, the warrior king who smote Midian, reigns in Avith).

themes: Hadad the son of Bedad, who smote Midian in the field of Moab, reigned in his stead
and the name of his city was Avith
the fourth king, the warrior Hadad who smote Midian
a glimpse of Edom active and strong, a worldly power in its prime

schools.summary: Gill: Hadad died, and Samlah of Masrekah reigned in his stead, about the supposed date; the witnesses note Hadad dying and Samlah of Masrekah reigning in his stead — the fifth king of Edom, again of another city (the line going on: 'Hadad died, and Samlah of Masrekah reigned in his stead'; the fifth king, 'Samlah of Masrekah,' who reigned after the warrior Hadad, again of yet another city, 'Masrekah'; the unbroken pattern, that each king is named with his city, and each of a different place, the crown still passing from one to another without dynasty, the elective monarchy continuing; the brevity of the record for these later kings, the names and cities given, little more, the bare succession marking the passing of the generations in Edom; that these kings reigned through long years while the chosen people grew slowly, the contrast between Edom's early and worldly greatness and Israel's hidden, appointed rise, so Samlah of Masrekah reigns in Hadad's stead).

themes: And Hadad died, and Samlah of Masrekah reigned in his stead
Samlah of Masrekah, the fifth king, again of another city
the unbroken pattern of the elective monarchy
the generations passing in Edom while Israel grew slowly

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: 'Rehoboth by the river' is in Hebrew Rehoboth-of-the-river, so called perhaps to distinguish it from another Rehoboth, the river being perhaps the Euphrates or a river of Edom; Gill: Samlah died, and Saul of Rehoboth by the river reigned in his stead; the witnesses note Samlah dying and Saul of Rehoboth by the river reigning in his stead — the sixth king of Edom, of Rehoboth by the river (the line continuing: 'Samlah died, and Saul of Rehoboth by the river reigned in his stead'; the sixth king, 'Saul of Rehoboth by the river,' again of another city; the place 'Rehoboth by the river,' as Ellicott explains, Rehoboth-of-the-river, perhaps so named to mark it as on a river, distinguishing it from another Rehoboth; the continued pattern of the elective kingship, each king of a different city, the crown moving on; that this 'Saul,' the same name as Israel's first king, was a king of Edom long before Israel had its Saul, a small token of Edom's earlier rise; the steady, unadorned recording of the line, so Saul of Rehoboth reigns in Samlah's stead).

themes: And Samlah died, and Saul of Rehoboth by the river reigned in his stead
Saul of Rehoboth by the river, the sixth king
a Saul of Edom long before Israel had its Saul
a token of Edom's earlier rise

schools.summary: Gill: Saul died, and Baal-hanan the son of Achbor reigned in his stead, about the supposed date; the witnesses note Saul dying and Baal-hanan the son of Achbor reigning in his stead — the seventh king of Edom (the line continuing toward its end: 'Saul died, and Baalhanan the son of Achbor reigned in his stead'; the seventh king, 'Baalhanan the son of Achbor,' reigning after Saul; the name 'Baal-hanan,' meaning 'Baal is gracious' or 'lord of grace,' a name that, like many in Edom and the surrounding nations, carries the mark of the false god Baal, a token of the idolatry of these peoples, in contrast to the worship of the true God in the chosen line; that no city is here named for this king, the record growing briefer toward the end of the list; the steady succession, king following king, the worldly kingdom running its course, so Baal-hanan reigns in Saul's stead, the seventh king of Edom).

themes: And Saul died, and Baalhanan the son of Achbor reigned in his stead
Baal-hanan the son of Achbor, the seventh king
his name ('Baal is gracious') marking the idolatry of Edom
a token of the false worship against the chosen line

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: he is more correctly called Hadad in the Samaritan text and the Hebrew; the naming of his wife and her ancestry is unusual in this list; Gill: Baal-hanan died, and Hadar reigned, his city being Pau; the witnesses note Baal-hanan dying and Hadar reigning in his stead, his city Pau, with his wife Mehetabel, daughter of Matred, named — the eighth and last king of Edom, with the rare note of his queen's lineage (the record closing the list of kings: 'Hadar reigned in his stead: and the name of his city was Pau; and his wife's name was Mehetabel, the daughter of Matred, the daughter of Mezahab'; the eighth king, 'Hadar,' called Hadad in some texts as Ellicott notes, the last of the line, his city 'Pau'; the unusual detail given for this king alone, that 'his wife's name was Mehetabel, the daughter of Matred, the daughter of Mezahab,' her name and ancestry traced, which some take to mark him as the last and perhaps most notable, or as one with whom the line of kings ended; that, unlike the others, Hadar's death is not recorded here, in the parallel in 1 Chronicles it is, some gathering that the kingly line gave way again to the rule of dukes; that with Hadar the eight kings of Edom are complete, so Hadar reigns in Pau, his queen Mehetabel named).

themes: Hadar reigned in his stead: and the name of his city was Pau
his wife's name was Mehetabel, the daughter of Matred, the daughter of Mezahab
Hadar, the eighth and last king of Edom
the rare note of his queen marking him the last, after whom dukes again ruled

schools.summary: Gill: after the regal monarchy ceased, the government in Edom was again by dukes, here named by their families and the places they ruled; the witnesses note the dukes of Esau named according to their families, places, and names, duke Timnah, duke Alvah, duke Jetheth — the second listing of the dukes, now by their territories, the rule returning to chieftains after the kings (the record giving a further list of dukes: 'these are the names of the dukes that came of Esau, according to their families, after their places, by their names; duke Timnah, duke Alvah, duke Jetheth'; the difference of this list from the earlier, that these dukes are reckoned not by their persons only but 'according to their families, after their places, by their names,' by the territories and districts over which they ruled, the names being of places as well as of men; the explanation the commentators give, that 'after the regal monarchy ceased,' the eight kings being past, 'the government in Edom was again by dukes,' the rule returning to chieftains of districts; that this list, naming the dukedoms by their places, marks the later organisation of Edom into its provinces, so the dukes of Esau are named again by their families and places).

themes: these are the names of the dukes that came of Esau, according to their families, after their places
duke Timnah, duke Alvah, duke Jetheth
the dukes of Esau named again by their families and places
the government returning to chieftains of districts after the kings

schools.summary: Gill: 'Aholibamah' is here the name of a man and of a place of which he was duke, Jerome observing that the names were sometimes of districts; the witnesses note the dukes Aholibamah, Elah, and Pinon, named among the dukedoms of Edom — the continuing of the list of dukedoms, the names now of districts as of men (the record naming three more dukes: 'Duke Aholibamah, duke Elah, duke Pinon'; the names, 'Aholibamah' here a duke and a place, though earlier the name of Esau's wife, the commentators noting that in this list the names are of districts or dukedoms as well as of men, the same name serving for a person and the territory he ruled; 'Elah' and 'Pinon,' likewise names of dukedoms in Edom, 'Pinon' thought to be the place known for its mines; that this list, unlike the first list of dukes by their persons, gives the dukedoms by their settled places, marking the provinces into which Edom was now divided; the orderly recording of the territories of Edom, so the dukes Aholibamah, Elah, and Pinon are named by their places).

themes: Duke Aholibamah, duke Elah, duke Pinon
three more dukedoms, the names of districts as of men
the same name serving for person and territory
marking the provinces into which Edom was divided

schools.summary: Gill: there was a Kenaz the son of Eliphaz, and a Teman his son, who were both dukes, but these seem to be different from them, named here as dukedoms after their places; the witnesses note the dukes Kenaz, Teman, and Mibzar among the dukedoms of Edom — the continuing of the territorial dukedoms, bearing names known from the earlier line (the record naming three more: 'Duke Kenaz, duke Teman, duke Mibzar'; that 'Kenaz' and 'Teman' had been names among the sons of Eliphaz and the earlier dukes, and the commentators weigh whether these are the same or different, most taking them as dukedoms here named after their places, which had taken the names of those early chiefs, the districts called by the names of their founders; 'Mibzar,' meaning 'fortress' or 'stronghold,' a dukedom perhaps named from a fortified place; that this confirms the nature of the second list, the dukedoms named by their territories, the old family names now become the names of provinces; the orderly completing of the dukedoms of Edom, so the dukes Kenaz, Teman, and Mibzar are named).

themes: Duke Kenaz, duke Teman, duke Mibzar
three more dukedoms bearing names from the earlier line
the districts called by the names of their founding chiefs
the dukedoms named by their territories

schools.summary: Gill: Magdiel, according to Jerome, was in the country of Gabalena, formerly possessed by the dukes of Edom, and the Targum of Jonathan adds a note on this duke; the witnesses note the dukes Magdiel and Iram, the last of the dukes of Edom, with the closing summary that Esau is the father of the Edomites — the sealing of the whole genealogy, all gathered into the one truth that Esau is the father of Edom (the chapter closing with the last dukes and a final word: 'Duke Magdiel, duke Iram: these be the dukes of Edom, according to their habitations in the land of their possession: he is Esau the father of the Edomites'; the last two dukes, 'Magdiel' and 'Iram,' completing the dukedoms 'according to their habitations in the land of their possession,' each ruling his district in the land Edom held; the grand closing summary, 'he is Esau the father of the Edomites,' the fourth and final pressing of the truth that runs through the chapter, that the whole nation of Edom, with all its sons, dukes, kings, and provinces, sprang from one man, Esau; the lesson the whole chapter teaches, the faithfulness of God, that Esau, though the rejected brother, was yet made a great and ordered nation exactly as God had said; that, this done, the history now returns to follow Jacob and the chosen line, so the dukes of Edom are completed and all sealed in the word, he is Esau the father of the Edomites).

debates.question: What does the whole genealogy of Genesis 36 ultimately teach?

sides.position: The faithfulness of God — that Esau, though the rejected brother, was yet made a great and ordered nation (sons, dukes, kings, provinces), exactly as God had promised, the whole sprung from one man

themes: Duke Magdiel, duke Iram: these be the dukes of Edom
he is Esau the father of the Edomites
the sealing of the whole genealogy, all sprung from Esau
the faithfulness of God in making the rejected brother a great nation
